Mr. Widerhofer, MMag., was selected by a top-class panel of experts in the current issue of the real estate magazine as one of the best real estate lawyers in Austria!

Chaos surrounding rent reduction

By Stefan Posch, immoflash today, March 25, 2020.
Currently, numerous articles, newsletters, and commentaries on the topic of rent reduction claims are circulating. Justice Minister Alma Zadić has also publicly expressed her legal opinion on the matter. However, it is not entirely clear whether the landlord alone must bear the costs in the event of an official closure.
“The legal opinion that the coronavirus and the associated restrictions release tenants from their obligation to pay rent is currently widely reported in the public media. This legal opinion is by no means accurate in its general form and, if applied without careful consideration, can have significant consequences for the tenant,” states an analysis by lawyer Herbert Gartner. The crucial question is whether the current situation, in which certain activities in specific “premises” are prohibited by law, even falls under the “concept of epidemic” in Section 1104 of the Austrian Civil Code (ABGB). According to Gartner, this is not the case; the impairment of use does not result from a “epidemic” but only from a specific prohibition on certain professional groups working in a specific way.

Erwin Widerhofer and Thomas Hafner of the Vienna law firm ENGIN DENIZ , in an interview with immoflash, believe the definition of an epidemic is met. “We do believe that Section 1104 of the Austrian Civil Code (ABGB) applies. However, the extent to which the property is affected must be decided on a case-by-case basis,” the lawyers stated. For example, closed business premises could still be used as storage, or restaurants could continue to make deliveries. They recommend that landlords check and document the possibilities for use. It should also be noted whether the lease contains an exclusion clause for Sections 1104 and 1105 of the Austrian Civil Code (ABGB).

Georg Edlauer, chairman of the Austrian Federal Economic Chamber’s (WKO) professional association of real estate and asset trustees, also notes differing legal opinions on this issue, although a unified perspective is slowly emerging. He clarifies that the Justice Minister’s position is just one of several. “Legal clarity will likely only come with a Supreme Court ruling, at the earliest next year. However, we advocate for a timely and individualized approach to reaching a consensus between tenants and landlords. It is a thousand times more beneficial for the tenant to survive economically than for them to be forced into bankruptcy, leaving the commercial space vacant,” he points out. “We also mustn’t lose sight of the fact that we will be in a recessionary economic environment after the coronavirus crisis,” he adds.

Anton Holzapfel emphasized to immoflash that the real estate industry alone cannot bear the burden of providing relief to businesses and retailers. “We at the Austrian Real Estate Association (ÖVI) are also working to raise this issue with policymakers,” Holzapfel stated. Legally, the issue of rent reductions under Section 1104 of the Austrian Civil Code (ABGB) is also relatively uncharted territory. “There is one ruling from 1953 concerning the confiscation of a rental property by one of the victorious powers of World War II. Before that, there was another from around 1905. A comparable case has not occurred since, and therefore has not been adjudicated,” Holzapfel explained.

Furthermore, it’s true that together we can do it.

media

Precedent-setting ruling or case-by-case decision?

The Supreme Court’s decision 5 Ob 74/17v of November 20, 2017, is still widely discussed and causing considerable consternation for many legal practitioners. Whether this ruling truly deserves such prominence and whether the legal situation regarding location-based surcharges has actually changed as a result of this decision are the questions that will be briefly examined here.
Dr. Rudolf Hauswirth

ENGIN DENIZ Newsletter 2018-01

NGIN DENIZ – 60 Years of Tradition

Sixty years ago, our law firm was founded in Vienna by Dr. Gerhard Engin-Deniz. While his passion lay in trademark and competition law, the firm was already active in real estate law at that time. We have built our clear focus on this foundation. ENGIN DENIZ is the law firm for real estate law – exclusively real estate law. For a long time, our founder was a typical solo practitioner; this only changed with the addition of Dr. Reimitz. Today, with Dr. Reimitz, Mag. Hafner, Dr. Hauswirth, and MMag. Widerhofer, we have four partners, and a total of more than ten lawyers work at ENGIN DENIZ – Attorneys at Law for Real Estate Law.

To celebrate our long-standing existence, we would like to extend a warm invitation to our anniversary celebration and ask you to save the date: Wednesday, October 3, 2018, 6:00 p.m.

Real Estate Focus

media


Miscarriage of justice or appeal to the legislature?
At the beginning of the year, the Supreme Court (OGH) published a far-reaching decision on the topic of location-based surcharges. This decision is a miscarriage of justice. The OGH’s interpretation is untenable under both a historical and a teleological interpretation.

Author: Christian Reimitz

ENGIN DENIZ Newsletter-02

media

New contract templates for property managers and real estate agents.
In 2017, we were able to contribute as legal advisors to two important model contracts in the industry.

Property Management Agreement Update:
Ten years ago, we developed the first template for a property management agreement in cooperation with the Austrian Association of Property Managers (ÖVI). After ten years, it was time to update and further develop this template. The 2017 update has now been published on the ÖVI website and is available there for members. Our clients can request the template directly from our office.

ENGIN DENIZ Newsletter-01

media

ENGIN DENIZ are the “lawyers specializing in real estate law.”
With five lawyers and a total of 10 legal professionals, our team is the size necessary to address the diverse aspects of real estate law through internal specialization. Real estate projects can involve the interplay of developer contract law, condominium ownership law, and landlord-tenant law. Construction law, construction contract law, and corporate law are equally important areas of law.
To achieve the highest quality across this wide range of legal fields, we have focused on real estate. We are convinced that this specialization was the right decision for our firm, as it allows us to better support our clients with their legal matters.

ÖVI NEWS

media

What’s changing regarding inheritance?
The majority of the 2015 Inheritance Law Amendment Act comes into force on January 1, 2017. The changes also have implications for real estate law.

By MMag. Erwin Widerhofer

International award

Awards

The expert group “Real Estate Law Experts” recommends Dr. Christian Reimitz as the best real estate lawyer in Austria for 2015/16. The international, London-based expert group “Real Estate Law Experts” lists Dr. Reimitz as the best real estate lawyer in Austria.
Real Estate Law Experts selects the best real estate law specialists worldwide. Experts are invited for nomination based on in-depth research, client surveys, and recommendations within the network.
We are proud of our international recognition and thank the Real Estate Law Experts!

Caesar Real Estate Award 2014

Awards

As in the two previous years, Dr. Christian Reimitz was once again voted the best lawyer by winning the overall public vote!

Star Cocktail No. 2

Events

We called the housewarming party for our old office on Sterngasse the “Star Cocktail.” The celebration remained a sparkling memory and became a tradition. The second Star Cocktail needed no rational explanation whatsoever. The sheer joy of celebrating is reason enough.
